title: Mughlai chicken
subtitle: Chicken in a cream, almond, and saffron sauce
excerpt: Chicken thighs cooked in a rich, pale sauce of cream, ground almonds, yoghurt, and saffron with a base of slow-cooked spiced onion. The Mughal court dish that reached Anglo-Indian tables, aromatic, mild, and luxurious in texture.
sourceNotes: Based on Mughlai cooking traditions documented in Salma Husain, 'The Emperor's Table: The Art of Mughal Cuisine' (2008) and Madhur Jaffrey, 'A Taste of India' (1985). Mughlai cuisine is the cooking of the Mughal court of northern India, which shaped the entire tradition of restaurant Indian cooking across South Asia and the diaspora.

content.text: Mughlai chicken belongs to the northern Indian court cooking that peaked under the Mughal emperors of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. The hallmark of Mughlai cooking is richness: cream, clarified butter, dried fruit, nuts, and saffron used with a careful hand. The dish is mild in heat but complex in flavour. The base is a slow-cooked onion paste thickened with ground almonds, not flour or lentils.

The technique requires patience at the onion stage: the onions must become deeply golden, almost caramelised, before anything else is added. This caramelised onion base (called browned onion paste in restaurant kitchens) is the foundation of the sauce and cannot be rushed.

items.name: Saffron
items.prepNote: steeped in 2 tbsp warm milk for 10 minutes

content.text: Steep the {{saffron}} in 2 tablespoons of warm milk. Heat {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over a medium heat. Add {{cardamom-pods}} lightly crushed cardamom pods, {{cloves}} cloves, and the {{cinnamon-stick}}, fry for 30 seconds. Add {{onion}} sliced onion and cook slowly for 20 to 25 minutes until deeply golden. Add {{garlic}} and {{ginger-fresh}} grated ginger and cook for 3 more minutes.

Add {{chicken-thigh}} of chicken thighs and stir to coat in the onion base. Cook for 5 minutes over a medium heat. Whisk {{full-fat-yoghurt}} of yoghurt with {{ground-almonds}} of ground almonds and add to the pan. Stir and reduce the heat to low. Add the saffron and its soaking milk. Cover and cook for 25 to 30 minutes until the chicken is cooked through, stirring occasionally. Stir in {{double-cream}} of double cream and {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Heat through gently for 3 minutes without boiling. Serve with basmati rice and warmed flatbread.

items.fix: Reduce the heat to low before adding the yoghurt-almond mixture and stir continuously. Full-fat yoghurt splits less readily than low-fat.
items.cause: Temperature too high when yoghurt is added

items.fix: Always whisk the ground almonds into the yoghurt before adding to the pan. This prevents them forming lumps.
items.cause: Ground almonds not blended with yoghurt before adding

items.fix: Add a splash of warm water and stir. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the chicken but pourable.
items.cause: Ground almonds absorbing too much liquid

content.text: Mughlai cooking took shape in the imperial kitchens of Delhi and Agra from the sixteenth century. Persian and Central Asian food traditions merged with the spice palette of the Indian subcontinent. The result was rich, fragrant, and often cream- and nut-enriched. It became the template for restaurant Indian cooking across South Asia and, in time, in Britain. Dishes like chicken tikka masala and korma are distant cousins. Mughlai chicken as a named dish appears in Anglo-Indian cookery books of the twentieth century as a shorthand for the style: mild, fragrant, and made for guests.
